key_insight: The paper proposes OSET, where plastic deformation starts from lattice-free oblate spheroidal transformation zones that can collectively reproduce classical dislocations as an emergent limit.
engineering_relevance: This could unify plasticity modeling across crystals, glasses, ceramics, grain boundaries, and nanocrystals using a single constitutive framework that does not depend on a periodic lattice.
method: Model each elementary plastic event as a shear-eigenstrained oblate spheroid (OSTZ) using Eshelby inclusion theory.
Show that a single OSTZ gives a finite, non-singular elastic dipole rather than a dislocation singularity.
Demonstrate that chains of OSTZs map to Peierls–Nabarro dislocations and that dislocations appear only beyond a critical host-lattice-dependent length.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation against grain-boundary-sliding data plus literature across metals, ceramics, and bulk metallic glasses, including a 41-system compilation, with dilatational eigenstrains matched within 2% and shear eigenstrains within 15%.
watch_item: The framework is theory-heavy and appears parameter-sensitive through fitted eigenstrains, so broad predictive robustness will depend on independent validation beyond the cited datasets.
paper_title: Oblate Spheroid Excitation Theory: A Unified, Lattice-Free Foundation for Plastic Deformation from Which Dislocations Emerge as Collective Excitations
